title: Muckle-Mouth Meg
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Frowned the Laird on the Lord: ן¿½So, red-handed I catch thee?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Death-doomed by our Law of the Border!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ve a gallows outside and a chiel to dispatch thee:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who trespasses, hangs: allן¿½s in order.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He met frown with smile, did the young English gallant:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the Lairdן¿½s dame: ן¿½Nay, Husband, I beg!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s comely: be merciful! Grace for the callant
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If he marries our Muckle-mouth Meg!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No mile-wide-mouthed monster of yours do I marry:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grant rather the gallows!ן¿½ laughed he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Foul fare kith and kin of you, why do you tarry?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To tame your fierce temper!ן¿½ quoth she.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shove him quick in the Hole, shut him fast for a week:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cold, darkness, and hunger work wonders:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who lion-like roars now, mouse-fashion will squeak,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½it rainsן¿½ soon succeed to ן¿½it thunders.ן¿½ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A week did he bide in the cold and the dark
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not hunger: for duly at morning
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In flitted a lass, and a voice like a lark
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Chirped, ן¿½Muckle-mouth Meg still yeן¿½re scorning?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go hang, but hereן¿½s parritch to hearten ye first!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Did Megן¿½s muckle-mouth boast within some
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such music as yours, mine should match it or burst:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No frog-jaws! So tell folk, my Winsome!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soon week came to end, and, from Holeן¿½s door set wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out he marched, and there waited the lassie:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yon gallows, or Muckle-mouth Meg for bride!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Consider! Skyן¿½s blue and turfן¿½s grassy:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lifeן¿½s sweet: shall I say ye wed Muckle-mouth Meg?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Not I,ן¿½ quoth the stout heart: ן¿½too eerie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mouth that can swallow a bubblyjockן¿½s egg;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall I let it munch mine? Never, Dearie!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Not Muckle-mouth Meg? Wow, the obstinate man!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps he would rather wed me!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ay, would he, with just for a dowry your can!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½m Muckle-mouth Meg,ן¿½ chirruped she.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then so-so-so-so,ן¿½ as he kissed her apace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Will I widen thee out till thou turnest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From Margaret Minnikin-mouן¿½, by Godן¿½s grace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Muckle-mouth Meg in good earnest!ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
